summary: When a guard checks counts, timing, or active-item limits before a write, look for TOCTOU or race-condition bugs and say whether the check must move into the same transaction, mutex, or serialized path as the write.
When optimistic state is updated before an async action, verify rollback happens on thrown exceptions as well as returned error objects, or stale UI and durable state can drift apart.
For low-level helpers and C or systems-style code, check for NULL or invalid parameter guards before dereference or use, and call out inconsistent return-value handling.
After reset, cleanup, or external-storage failure paths, verify durable status and error fields no longer claim success or completion when the resource is actually gone or failed.
When code consults a transient existence check or derived field, compare it against the persisted source of truth and call out mismatches that can ignore real stored state.

When cleanup or teardown depends on best-effort metadata that is only logged and not actually required by the API contract, call out the bug if a temporary metadata failure can skip the real cleanup request.

When code launches a detached process or async helper and then verifies startup once after a fixed sleep, check for false-failure races where slow startup is treated as a hard failure without retries or a durable readiness signal.

When file metadata or object existence checks accept a persisted bucket or storage location, verify later reads and signed URLs use that same persisted location instead of silently falling back to the current default bucket.

For shared async rate-limit or throttle helpers, check whether the last-request timestamp or similar coordination state is only updated after await or fetch completes, which allows concurrent callers to bypass the intended serialization.

When a connector or gateway receives a non-2xx response, verify the response body or structured failure payload is preserved through normalization instead of collapsed into a generic status-only error.

For connector/workflow boundary changes, check wrapped payload shapes against the real returned object, bound external awaits with timeout or abort behavior, preserve explicit null or cleared state against fallbacks, and compute wake or pending status from the current run rather than global pending state.
